name: Silver Hill
short_description: Gentle-slope rockhounding site near Spokane featuring quartz and agate specimens in accessible terrain. Year-round collecting opportunity in the Columbia Plateau region.
description:
Silver Hill sits in Spokane County at 2,837 feet elevation within the Columbia Plateau physiographic province. This location offers gentle slopes and relatively accessible terrain for rockhounders of all skill levels seeking quartz and chalcedony specimens. The area's geological setting reflects Miocene flood basalt activity followed by weathering and erosion that exposed silica-rich mineral deposits typical of eastern Washington's volcanic geology.

The site provides opportunities to collect milky quartz, chalcedony fragments, and occasional agate nodules weathered from the surrounding basalt formations. Collectors should focus on exposed rocky slopes, wash areas, and areas where previous weathering has concentrated mineral specimens. The gentle terrain makes this location suitable for family outings and beginner rockhounders exploring eastern Washington's mineral diversity.

Eastern Washington's arid climate keeps the ground relatively dry and accessible throughout much of the year. The Spokane area sits in the rain shadow east of the Cascade Mountains, creating stable collecting conditions. Visitors should bring adequate water and sun protection, as the open terrain offers limited shade.

This location represents typical Plateau country rockhounding - straightforward mineral collection without technical difficulty. The combination of accessible terrain, moderate specimen quality, and proximity to Spokane makes Silver Hill a practical choice for regular collecting trips.
history: Silver Hill sits within the Columbia Plateau, where extensive Miocene flood basalt eruptions (17-14 million years ago) blanketed eastern Washington with thick basalt flows. Subsequent weathering and groundwater percolation created secondary silica deposits, including quartz and chalcedony crystalline phases that fill fractures and form nodules within and atop the basalt sequence.
tips: Search exposed rocky slopes and drainage washes where weathering has concentrated mineral fragments - look for white, translucent, or banded quartz and chalcedony pieces. Bring a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from weathered outcrops without damaging surrounding material. Focus on the base of slopes and wash bottoms where heavier mineral material accumulates. Early morning collecting offers better visibility and cooler temperatures on the open, sunny terrain.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,837 feet with gentle slopes suitable for most hikers. Rocky, semi-arid terrain with sparse sagebrush and grass cover typical of the Columbia Plateau. Walking distance from parking to collecting areas varies but generally moderate. Footing is stable on exposed bedrock and consolidated soil, though some loose rock and uneven ground present. No significant obstacles or technical terrain.
best_season: April - October for optimal conditions. Mild spring temperatures make April-May ideal for full-day collecting trips. Summer (June-August) offers reliable weather but requires early morning starts and afternoon shade breaks due to heat. Fall (September-October) provides cool comfortable conditions without spring mud. Winter (November-March) is possible but occasional snow and ice can limit access.
written_directions: From Spokane, take US Highway 395 north approximately 8-10 miles toward Deer Park. Look for local roads or a marked pullout on the east side of the highway in the Silver Hill vicinity. GPS coordinates 47.559173, -117.327674 pinpoint the site. Access may require permission from private landowners depending on exact parcel boundaries - verify access status before visiting.
road_conditions: Paved highway access to the general area. Local access roads and pullouts may be unpaved but passable for standard vehicles.
parking_notes: Pullout or roadside parking likely available. Capacity and facilities unknown - plan for self-sufficient parking situation typical of public roadside collecting areas.
collection_rules: Verify land ownership and access permission before collecting. If on public land, standard personal-use rockhounding regulations apply. Do not trespass on private property. Collect only hand-portable specimens. No motorized equipment or excavation.
rules:
Verify ownership and get permission before collecting
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools and portable collection only
Leave the site as you found it
nearest_services: Spokane, approximately 8-10 miles south - full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved access roads may exist but paved highway proximity ensures vehicle access. Sturdy footwear recommended for rocky terrain. Limited shade and exposed sun make this location challenging in peak summer heat for those with heat sensitivity. Generally suitable for visitors with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find at Silver Hill near Spokane?
faq.answer: Silver Hill produces quartz, chalcedony, and agate specimens typical of the Columbia Plateau basalt region. Collectors find milky quartz pieces, translucent chalcedony fragments, and occasionally banded agate nodules weathered from the Miocene basalt formations. Quality and size vary, but hand-portable specimens are reliably available.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Silver Hill free and what permits do I need?
faq.answer: No permits are required for personal-use rockhounding at Silver Hill. However, verify land ownership before collecting - access depends on whether you are on public land or private property. If privately owned, obtain permission from the landowner. Collection is free for personal use only; commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Silver Hill Washington?
faq.answer: April through October offers the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) provide ideal temperatures without summer heat or winter weather obstacles. Summer is possible with early morning starts. Winter can bring occasional snow or ice that limits access, though the arid climate keeps most of the season dry.

faq.question: How difficult is collecting at Silver Hill for beginners?
faq.answer: Silver Hill is excellent for beginning rockhounders. The gentle slopes, accessible terrain, and close proximity to Spokane make it beginner-friendly. No technical skills or specialized equipment required beyond basic hand tools. Families with children age 6+ can participate comfortably. The straightforward geology of quartz and chalcedony specimens makes identification easy for newcomers.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring when rockhounding near Spokane at Silver Hill?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer and chisel for extracting specimens from weathered outcrops. A collection bag or bucket for specimens is essential. Sun protection including a hat and sunscreen is critical on the exposed terrain. Bring at least 2-3 liters of water per person, as the site offers limited shade and the semi-arid climate causes rapid dehydration. Sturdy hiking boots protect against uneven rocky footing.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and dog rockhounding to Silver Hill?
faq.answer: Yes, Silver Hill is family-friendly with easy terrain suitable for children age 6 and older. Dogs are welcome on public land. The gentle slopes and accessible parking make it convenient for full-day family outings. Plan for water, snacks, and sun protection. The open terrain offers good visibility for supervising children and pets. Verify access status and any private property boundaries before arriving.

meta_description: Find quartz, chalcedony, and agate at Silver Hill near Spokane. Easy public rockhounding on gentle terrain. No permits required for personal collection.
